content.body:
# Implementation plan

KI-generierte Dashboards (Prompt-to-Dashboard)
Ziel ist es, den Athleten zu ermöglichen, sich personalisierte Dashboards mithilfe von Texteingaben (Prompts) zusammenzustellen. Das System muss verstehen, welche sportwissenschaftlichen Metriken, Visualisierungsarten und Zeiträume der User sehen möchte, und daraus ein dynamisches Layout generieren.

User Review Required
IMPORTANT

Das Backend nutzt NestJS, Prisma, Langchain und Zod. Structured Outputs sind daher ideal mit Zod und Langchain realisierbar.
Sollen die Dashboards als persistente Views via Prisma (z.B. neues Model Dashboard) gespeichert werden oder rein flüchtig sein?
Es existieren aktuell keine Metrik-Endpunkte im Backend. Ein neuer Service (DashboardMetricsService) muss von Grund auf gebaut werden.
1. Backend Konzept (NestJS, Prisma, Langchain)
Damit das Frontend flexibel Graphen rendern kann, muss das Backend in NestJS zwei wesentliche Komponenten bereitstellen: 1. Die Config-Generierung via LLM und 2. Die Datenquellen-Abfrage.

A. Datenstrukturen (DTOs)
Das Backend muss eine standardisierte Beschreibung für Dashboards und Widgets definieren:

typescript
import { z } from "zod";
// Zod Schema für Structured Output vom LLM
export const WidgetConfigSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  type: z.enum(["LINE_CHART", "BAR_CHART", "SINGLE_VALUE", "RADAR_CHART"]),
  // Das LLM wählt aus vordefinierten Metriken, es erfindet keine Daten!
  metricTypes: z.array(z.string()), // z.B. ['RESTING_HEART_RATE', 'TRAINING_LOAD']
  timeframe: z.enum(["7_DAYS", "30_DAYS", "12_WEEKS", "1_YEAR"]),
  aggregation: z.enum(["AVG", "MAX", "SUM", "LATEST"]),
  title: z.string(),
});
export const DashboardConfigSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  title: z.string(),
  description: z.string().optional(),
  widgets: z.array(WidgetConfigSchema),
});
// Inferierte Typen für Prisma/Services
export type DashboardConfigDto = z.infer<typeof DashboardConfigSchema>;
export type WidgetConfigDto = z.infer<typeof WidgetConfigSchema>;
B. Neue API Endpunkte
POST /api/dashboards/generate
Controller: Nimmt den User-Prompt entgegen.
Service (DashboardAiService):
Nutzt @langchain/openai oder @langchain/google-vertexai zusammen mit .withStructuredOutput(DashboardConfigSchema).
Wichtig zur Agenten-Freiheit: Der Agent generiert nicht die echten Datenzeilen (z.B. Heartrate=65 am 12.03.). Das LLM baut nur das strukturelle "Rezept" (Das JSON Schema oben). Er entscheidet aufgrund des Prompts frei: "Ah, der User will einen Line-Chart mit der HRV der letzten 30 Tage".
Das generierte Dashboard JSON "Rezept" kann danach (optional) in der DB via Prisma persistiert werden, um es später abzurufen.
GET /api/dashboards & GET /api/dashboards/:id
Normale Prisma CRUD Operationen für persistierte Dashboards.
POST /api/dashboards/metrics/query (Der "Data Resolver")
Hier passiert die Magie: Das Frontend schickt das vom LLM generierte "Rezept" (WidgetConfigDto) an diesen Endpunkt.
Service (DashboardMetricsService):
Dieser klassische (nicht-KI) Backend-Service liest das Rezept: type: LINE_CHART, metric: HRV, timeframe: 30_DAYS.
Er holt dann die echten, validen Daten aus der Datenbank (über Prisma) für den User.
Das Backend gibt diese "echten" Daten an das Frontend zurück, welches sie stumm in die Chart.js Komponente kippt.
2. Frontend Vorbereitungen
Das Frontend fungiert in diesem System als "Renderer" für die Backend-Konfiguration.

A. Neue Komponenten
NativePromptDashboard.vue (Screen): Ein View, in dem der User entweder fertige Dashboards wählt oder einen Prompt für ein neues eintippt.
DashboardRenderer.vue: Nimmt ein DashboardConfigDto als Prop entgegen und rendert ein CSS-Grid oder eine Flex-List der Widgets.
Widget Komponenten (WidgetLineChart.vue, WidgetSingleMetric.vue): Diese erhalten ein einzelnes WidgetConfigDto. Im onMounted rufen sie den API-Endpunkt /api/metrics/query für exakt diese Konfiguration auf, holen die Time-Series-Daten und übergeben sie an Chart.js.
B. State Management
useCustomDashboards.ts: Ein neuer Pinia-Composable, um die Benutzerdashboards zu laden, zu speichern und zu verwalten (siehe State Management Rules: Singleton Pattern).
Verification Plan
Da wir hier reines Konzepting betreiben (vor der Umsetzung):

Automated Tests (Später)
Backend Unit-Tests: Mock LLM Responses und prüfe, ob das Backend falsche JSON-Strukturen ordnungsgemäß abfängt oder korrigiert (z.B. wenn das LLM eine Metrik nennt, die es im MetricEnum gar nicht gibt).
Backend Service-Tests: Der MetricsQueryService muss massiv getestet werden: Rechnet er Averages korrekt aus? Stimmt die Zeitzone?
Frontend Component Tests: Überprüfe, dass DashboardRenderer anhand von Mock-Config-Daten (z.B. 2 LineCharts, 1 SingleStat) die korrekten inneren Komponenten mounted.
Manual Verification
Eingabe diverser Edge-Case Prompts (z.B. "Zeig mir Daten, die ihr gar nicht habt") und prüfen der Systemstabilität.
Geschwindigkeitstest: Ladezeiten der aggregierten Metriken bei 1 Jahr Historie.

content.body:
## Problem

Currently, agents always produce a response to every user message, even when the conversation has naturally concluded (e.g., user says "thanks", "bye", or the agent has fully answered the question). This can feel unnatural and lead to unnecessary LLM calls.

## Proposed Solution

Allow agents to decide to "not answer" and signal that the current conversation is over. This would:

1. Add a mechanism for the agent to indicate it believes the conversation is complete (e.g., a special tool call, a structured output field, or a stop signal)
2. When the agent signals completion, the system should stop the conversation loop and not generate further responses
3. The agent should use this judiciously — only when it's confident the user's needs have been met

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Agent can signal "conversation complete" during its turn
- [ ] System respects the signal and ends the conversation gracefully
- [ ] Signal is surfaced to the client so it can update UI accordingly
- [ ] Agent doesn't prematurely end conversations (needs good heuristics or explicit criteria)

content.body:
Ben is a coach. Right now, he doesn't behave like one.

A good coach doesn't just react to what happened today — they track patterns across weeks, notice when something is shifting, and bring it up before the athlete does. They remember that last week's training was heavy, that HRV has been trending down for ten days, that the athlete mentioned feeling off. That continuity is what makes coaching feel personal and credible.

Today, Ben has no memory between messages. Every wake-up message and post-activity message is generated from scratch, with only the immediate trigger as context. Ben sees last night's sleep and comments on it — but has no idea whether it's the tenth poor night in a row or the first. He sees a completed interval session — but doesn't know that this brings the athlete's acute training load to its highest point in eight weeks.

The user experience consequence is subtle but real: ****Ben sounds attentive, but isn't.**** Users who engage consistently start to notice the repetition. The coaching feels reactive rather than proactive, and the app feels like a summarizer rather than a training partner.

Deep Check-Ins is the feature that fixes this. It gives Ben pattern memory — the ability to notice trends across signals, connect dots across time, and surface the one insight a good coach would actually mention unprompted.

## Problem

LLMs by default are very friendly and act as pure executors of requests. YouB should mirror more of a human coach — a parental figure that is supportive and uses tools on the user's behalf where appropriate, but also **pushes back on bad training decisions and detrimental behavior**.

## Expected Behavior

The agent should:
- Be supportive and helpful by default
- Proactively use tools for the user when appropriate
- **Push back** when users make bad training decisions (e.g. overtraining, ignoring recovery, unrealistic goals)
- Act as a responsible coach, not just a yes-man that executes whatever is asked

content.body:
## Context: Adaptive Training Sessions

To move towards adaptive, personalized training sessions, our knowledge base plans need precise intensity definitions. Cycling sessions already use `ftpRange` (percentage of FTP) on workout parts for fine-grained power targets. Running sessions currently rely solely on zones (Z1-Z6), which map to hardcoded percentage bands of threshold pace — but lack the equivalent `paceRange` field for finer control.

## Current State

| Sport | KB Sessions | Sessions with fine-grained range | Coverage |
|-------|-------------|----------------------------------|----------|
| Cycling | 60 | 35 (with `ftpRange`) | **58%** |
| Running | 43 | 0 (with `paceRange`) | **0%** |

### How it works today

- **Cycling:** Workout parts can specify `ftpRange: { min: %, max: % }` relative to FTP. 35 of 60 cycling sessions already use this. The enricher resolves these to actual watt targets.
- **Running:** Workout parts have `zones` (Z1-Z6) which map to hardcoded percentage bands of threshold pace (e.g., Z3 → 105-112%). Parts _can_ also have an optional `paceRange: { slow: %, fast: % }` field for finer-grained control, and the enricher already handles it if present — but **no running KB session currently populates `paceRange`**.

### Key files

- `knowledge-base/running/sessions.json` — 43 running session templates (0 with paceRange)
- `knowledge-base/cycling/sessions.json` — 60 cycling session templates (35 with ftpRange)
- `src/training/utils/workout-structure-enricher.ts` — enrichment logic (already supports paceRange)
- `src/training/types/session-workout.types.ts` — PaceRange/FTPRange type definitions

## Proposal

1. **Audit** running sessions to identify which would benefit from paceRange (e.g., tempo runs at 103-106% vs the full Z3 band of 105-112%)
2. **Enrich** relevant running session workout parts with `paceRange` values in the KB
3. **Measure** improvement in pace target precision compared to zone-only defaults

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running KB sessions that have zone-spanning or sub-zone intensity targets are annotated with `paceRange`
- [ ] Coverage of `paceRange` in running sessions approaches cycling's ftpRange coverage (~58%)
- [ ] Existing enricher behavior is preserved (paceRange takes precedence over zone lookup when present)

## Labels

Enhancement — Knowledge Base — Training Plans
content.title: Enrich running KB sessions with paceRange for finer-grained pace targets

content.body:
## Problem

Suggestion generation sends the **full conversation history** to the LLM, consuming significant tokens for what produces a single short suggestion string. The current approach (`generate-suggestions.node.ts`) builds messages as:

1. System prompt (lengthy, explains perspective rules)
2. Full `conversationHistory` via `convertHistoryToMessages()`
3. Current user message
4. Generated AI response

For long conversations this is wasteful — most of the context is irrelevant for predicting the user's next message.

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Truncate history to last exchange only

Only include the last 2 messages (the most recent human message + agent response). The immediate context is what matters most for "what would the user say next?"

### 2. Role-swap trick

Instead of a system prompt explaining "generate suggestions from the USER's perspective" (which the current prompt has to emphasize heavily because the model naturally wants to respond as the assistant), **swap the message roles**:

- Last agent response → labeled as `HumanMessage`
- Last user message → labeled as `AIMessage`

Then instruct the model to "reply in max 5 words to continue the conversation."

The model now thinks it IS the user replying to the assistant, so it naturally produces what the user would say next. This eliminates the perspective confusion problem entirely and allows a much simpler, shorter prompt.

### 3. Single suggestion

We only display one suggestion in the UI, so reduce from 3 to 1. This also means we can potentially **drop structured output** and just use the raw text response (trimmed), further simplifying the call.

## Expected Impact

- **Token reduction**: ~80-90% fewer input tokens per suggestion call (no full history, minimal prompt)
- **Simpler prompt**: No need for elaborate perspective instructions
- **Faster**: Less input = lower latency
- **Potentially cheaper model**: With such a simple task, could even explore using a cheaper model tier

## Implementation Notes

- File: `src/agent/ben/graph/supervisor/nodes/generate-suggestions.node.ts`
- Prompt: `src/agent/ben/graph/conversation/prompts.ts` (`getSuggestionGenerationPrompt`)
- Schema: `SuggestionResponseSchema` — update to single string or remove if dropping structured output
- Update `chatSuggestions` state field and downstream consumers to handle single suggestion
- Update Langfuse prompt `conversation-suggestion-generation`
content.title: Optimize suggestion generation: reduce tokens with role-swap and single suggestion

content.body:
## Problem

Haptic feedback on Android is too strong and fires in places where it's not useful (e.g., navigation events, input focus). There's no consistent policy for when and how haptics should be used.

## Current State

~24 files use haptic feedback via the `useHaptics` composable. Current usage falls into two categories:

**Action feedback (appropriate):** button presses, toggles, confirmations, send message, voice recording, plan activation, pull-to-refresh completion, chart interactions

**Navigation/focus feedback (mostly inappropriate):**
- `NativeInput.vue` — haptic on focus
- `NativeDateInput.vue` — haptic on focus
- `NativeTextarea.vue` — haptic on focus
- `NativeSheet.vue` — haptic on sheet close
- `AppMobileBottomNav.vue` — haptic on tab switch (`selectionChanged`)
- `MobileThreeScreenNav.vue` — haptic on horizontal screen scroll
- `NativePullToRefresh.vue` — haptic on threshold crossing (before action)

## Proposed Policy

Haptics should signal that an **action** happened, not **navigation**. Specifically:

| Use Case | Haptic? | Style |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Button press (primary action) | Yes | Light |
| Destructive action confirm | Yes | Medium |
| Critical confirm (e.g. plan activation) | Yes | Heavy |
| Toggle / switch change | Yes | Light |
| Success / error outcome | Yes | Notification |
| Drag start / move / end | Yes | Selection API (light) |
| Input focus | **No** | — |
| Sheet open / close | **No** | — |
| Tab navigation | **No** | — |
| Screen scroll / swipe nav | **No** | — |
| Pull-to-refresh threshold bump | **No** | — |

## Tasks

- [ ] Remove haptic on input/textarea/date focus (`NativeInput`, `NativeDateInput`, `NativeTextarea`)
- [ ] Remove haptic on sheet close (`NativeSheet`)
- [ ] Remove haptic on bottom nav tab switch (`AppMobileBottomNav`)
- [ ] Remove haptic on screen scroll navigation (`MobileThreeScreenNav`)
- [ ] Remove haptic on pull-to-refresh threshold crossing (keep on trigger + completion)
- [ ] Audit remaining usages against the policy table above
- [ ] Verify on Android that remaining haptics feel appropriate in intensity
- [ ] Document the haptic policy in the composable or a shared guide

content.body:
## Why

Today, cycling activities get a **loadScore** (how hard was this workout on a universal scale) but running activities don't — they only get TRIMP (an HR-based proxy). This means we can't compare training load across sports, can't show runners how hard their workouts were relative to their fitness, and can't build weekly/plan load tracking for running-inclusive plans.

The math and the library function already exist in `@youbdev/sport-intelligence` — `calculateRunMetrics()` returns `tss` and `intensityFactor` when given a threshold pace. We just never wire it up.

## What

- **Pass the athlete's threshold pace** (from `AthleticProfile.thresholdPace`, converted from sec/km to m/s) to `calculateRunMetrics()` in the running analysis path
- **Fall back to 4.0 m/s (~4:10/km)** when threshold pace is null — same pattern as cycling's FTP default of 200W
- **Store the results** in `ActivityAnalysis.loadScore` and `ActivityAnalysis.intensityRatio` (fields already exist, currently null for running)
- **Backfill existing running activities**: Evolve the current `ACTIVITY_REANALYSIS_ENABLED` boolean into a numeric `ACTIVITY_REANALYSIS_DAYS` env var so we can control how far back to reprocess (e.g. `ACTIVITY_REANALYSIS_DAYS=90` to reprocess last 90 days)

## Context

- `RunMetrics` in the library already computes `normalizedGradedSpeed` (NGS, like Normalized Power for running), `intensityFactor` (NGS / threshold pace), and `tss` (load score)
- The running analysis path at `activity-analysis.service.ts:243` calls `analyzeRunningFromRecords()` but never passes threshold pace and never reads `tss`/`intensityFactor` from the result
- `thresholdPace` is stored as `Int` (sec/km) in `AthleticProfile`; library expects m/s → conversion: `1000 / thresholdPaceSecPerKm`
- Existing backfill pattern: `reanalyzeRecentActivities()` in `seed.service.ts` with boolean env flag

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Running activities produce a non-null `loadScore` and `intensityRatio` when analyzed
- [ ] Works with explicit threshold pace from athlete profile
- [ ] Falls back to 4.0 m/s default when threshold pace is null
- [ ] `loadScore` is visible in `ActivityAnalysisDto.power.loadScore` for running activities
- [ ] Backfill mechanism supports numeric days parameter
- [ ] Existing cycling loadScore computation is unaffected

content.body:
## Why

The `@youbdev/sport-intelligence` library uses TrainingPeaks-derived naming (`rTSS`, `rCTL`, `rATL`, `rTSB`) that is both trademark-sensitive and sport-specific ("r" prefix implies running-only). Since we're building a **unified load system** across cycling, running, and eventually other sports, the library should speak the same language as the server.

## What

In the `@youbdev/sport-intelligence` library (checked out at `../sport-intelligence`):

- Rename `rTSS` → `loadScore` everywhere
- Rename `rCTL` / `calculateR_CTL` → `CTL` / `calculateCTL`
- Rename `rATL` / `calculateR_ATL` → `ATL` / `calculateATL`  
- Rename `rTSB` / `calculateR_TSB` → `TSB` / `calculateTSB`
- Rename `calculateRunningLoadHistory` → `calculateLoadHistory` (it's just EMA math, not running-specific)
- The cycling equivalents (`calculateCyclingTrainingLoad` etc.) should use the same unified names
- Update `running-trainings-load.ts` and `cycling-trainings-load.ts` — consider merging into a single `training-load.ts` since the math is identical (42-day and 7-day EMA)

## Why not just rename at the consumer layer?

We control the library. Clean names at the source prevent every consumer from having to map terminology. It also avoids anyone accidentally using the old names thinking they're running-specific.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] No `rTSS`, `rCTL`, `rATL`, `rTSB` references remain in the library
- [ ] Single unified set of load functions that work for any sport's daily load scores
- [ ] Server consumers updated to use new names
- [ ] Library published with new version

content.body:
## Why

Training sessions have a `workoutStructure` that describes the workout in zone-relative terms (e.g. "4×8min at 91-105% FTP", "20min at DL2 pace"). But we never translate this into a concrete **planned loadScore** — the field exists on `TrainingSession` but is always null.

Without planned load, we can't answer basic questions:
- "How hard is this workout supposed to be?"
- "Did I go harder or easier than planned?"
- "What's my planned weekly load?"
- "Is this training week a build week or a recovery week?"

## What

Compute `loadScorePlanned` and `intensityRatioPlanned` **dynamically at read time** from:
- `workoutStructure` — warmup/mainSet/cooldown with zone targets and durations
- Athlete's current FTP (cycling) or threshold pace (running)
- `paceTargets` / `paceZones` for running sessions (populated for intensive sessions)
- `zonesUsed` as fallback for sessions without explicit pace targets

### Formula (per segment)

**Cycling:**
```
resolvedPower = midpoint(ftpRange) × athlete.ftp
segmentIF = resolvedPower / athlete.ftp
segmentLoadScore = (durationSeconds × segmentIF²) / 3600 × 100
```

**Running:**
```
resolvedPace = midpoint(paceRange) resolved via threshold pace
segmentIF = resolvedPace / thresholdPace  
segmentLoadScore = (durationSeconds × segmentIF²) / 3600 × 100
```

`plannedLoadScore = sum(all segments)`

### Why compute on read, not on write?

Planned load changes when the athlete's FTP or threshold pace changes. Computing at read time means it's always accurate without needing triggers to recalculate stored values. The computation is lightweight (iterate workout segments, multiply).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `TrainingSessionInfoDto.planned.loadScore` returns a computed value for sessions with workout structure
- [ ] Handles both cycling (FTP-based) and running (threshold pace-based) sessions
- [ ] Falls back to zone midpoints when explicit power/pace ranges aren't available
- [ ] Returns null gracefully when workout structure is missing or athlete has no profile
- [ ] Planned vs actual comparison is possible when activity is linked to session

## Depends on

- #135 (running loadScore for actual activities)
content.title: Compute planned loadScore dynamically from workout structure + athlete profile

content.body:
## Why

Individual activity load scores are useful, but the real value comes from **aggregation** — understanding training load over time. Athletes and coaches think in terms of:

- "Was this a hard week or a recovery week?" → **weekly load total**
- "Am I building fitness or stagnating?" → **CTL trend** (chronic training load, 42-day moving average)
- "Am I too fatigued to race well this weekend?" → **TSB** (training stress balance = fitness − fatigue)
- "Is my plan ramping safely?" → **ACWR** (acute:chronic workload ratio, should stay 0.8–1.3)

None of these are computable today despite having all the raw ingredients (per-activity loadScore).

## What

### Weekly & plan aggregation

- **Weekly actual load**: sum of `loadScore` from all completed activities in a plan week (by `weekNumber`)
- **Weekly planned load**: sum of planned loadScores from sessions in that week (depends on #137)
- **Plan total load**: sum across all weeks (planned and actual)
- **Completion %**: actual / planned per week

### Rolling fitness metrics

- **CTL** (Chronic Training Load): 42-day exponential moving average of daily loadScore
- **ATL** (Acute Training Load): 7-day EMA of daily loadScore
- **TSB** (Training Stress Balance): CTL − ATL (positive = fresh, negative = fatigued)
- **ACWR**: ATL / CTL (safe zone: 0.8–1.3)

### Open design questions

- **Storage strategy**: Compute on-the-fly from activity history, or materialize in a daily table? TBD based on performance testing.
- **Multi-sport aggregation**: Sum loadScores across sports (cycling + running) into unified daily total. Works because loadScore is calibrated to the same scale (~100/hr at threshold) for all sports.
- **Cold start**: What CTL to assume for new users with no history? Design doc suggests questionnaire-based estimation (descoped for now, default to 0).

## Use cases this enables

- **Plan view**: Show weekly load bars (planned vs actual) across the plan timeline
- **Session view**: "This session contributed 85 of your 350 weekly load target"
- **Dashboard**: Fitness/fatigue/form chart (CTL/ATL/TSB over time)
- **Plan generation**: The load trajectory builder (`buildWeeklyTSSTrajectory` from design docs) uses CTL as input to determine safe weekly load targets
- **Safety guardrails**: Flag weeks where ACWR > 1.3 or CTL ramp > 8/week
- **Adaptation triggers**: If actual CTL diverges significantly from projected, suggest plan adjustment

## References

- `docs/feature-designs/plan-generation/load-trajectory-design.md` — algorithm spec
- `docs/feature-designs/plan-generation/r3-ramp-rate.md` — CTL ramp rate thresholds
- `docs/feature-designs/plan-generation/r5-mesocycle-structure.md` — build/recovery rhythm
- `@youbdev/sport-intelligence` — `calculateNextEMA`, `calculateEMASeries` utilities

## Depends on

- #135 (running loadScore — need load across all sports)
- #136 (library rename — unified function names)
- #137 (planned loadScore — for planned vs actual comparison)

## Summary

When navigating back from settings sub-screens using the **Android system back gesture** (edge swipe), the incoming (revealed) page animates smoothly with the parallax effect, but the **leaving page disappears instantly** instead of sliding out to the right. The custom in-app swipe gesture works correctly with both pages animating.

## Root Cause Analysis

The issue is in how `pop()` interacts with `transition-group` when triggered via Android's back gesture vs the custom swipe gesture.

**Custom swipe (works correctly):**
- `NativeRoot.vue` tracks finger position via `swipeData`, applying inline `transform` during the drag
- On release past threshold, `pop()` is called but `swipeData` retains the offset for 500ms, keeping the leaving element positioned while `transition-group` runs its leave animation

**Android back gesture (broken):**
- Capacitor's `backButton` event fires (`back-button.client.ts:19`)
- `pop()` is called directly — no swipe data, no inline positioning
- `pop()` (`useNativeRouter.ts:40`) removes the item from the stack AND calls `window.history.back()` synchronously
- The `window.history.back()` call may cause Nuxt's router to react to the history change, triggering a re-render that interrupts the `transition-group` leave animation before it completes
- Result: the leaving screen vanishes instantly

## Relevant Files

- `components/native/NativeRoot.vue` — transition-group and swipe logic
- `composables/useNativeRouter.ts` — `pop()` implementation
- `plugins/back-button.client.ts` — Android back button handler

## Possible Fix Directions

1. **Decouple `pop()` from `history.back()`** — delay the `window.history.back()` call until after the transition completes (~500ms), or use `nextTick` to ensure Vue processes the transition-group leave before the history change
2. **Use a `popstate` listener** instead of calling `history.back()` inside `pop()` — let the Android gesture naturally pop history, and react to the `popstate` event to update the stack
3. **Add an `isPopping` flag** similar to `swipeData.isSnapping` that keeps the leaving element in the DOM with the transition active, independent of the history sync

## Test Plan

- [ ] Open settings, navigate to a sub-screen (e.g. Profile)
- [ ] Use Android system back gesture (edge swipe) to go back
- [ ] Verify leaving screen slides out to the right (same as custom swipe)
- [ ] Verify hardware back button also animates correctly
- [ ] Verify iOS custom swipe still works as before
- [ ] Verify `window.history` stays in sync (no stuck history entries)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Problem

WhatsApp message constraints (length limits, no formatting) are manually injected in each graph's nodes via `getWhatsAppConstraints()`. This is error-prone — the youb-expert subgraph is currently missing it entirely, and the activity + wakeup graphs (which always deliver via WhatsApp) also lack constraints.

The current `source` field semantics ("where did the request come from") don't cover proactive graphs (activity, wakeup) that have no user-initiated request but still deliver to WhatsApp.

## Proposal

1. **Rename the semantic from `source` to `channel`** — represents where the message will be *delivered*, not where it originated
2. **Extend `buildSystemMessages` / `buildCompleteMessageArray`** in `system-message-builder.ts` to accept an optional channel context (`{ channel, locale, i18nService }`) and auto-append WhatsApp constraints when `channel === 'whatsapp'`
3. **Migrate all message-producing graphs** to use the builder with channel context:
   - **conversation** — channel from request source (already has constraints, migrate to builder)
   - **training** — channel from request source (already has constraints, migrate to builder)
   - **youb-expert** — channel from request source (currently missing constraints)
   - **activity** — channel = `'whatsapp'` (proactive, always WhatsApp)
   - **wakeup** — channel = `'whatsapp'` (proactive, always WhatsApp)
4. **Remove standalone `getWhatsAppConstraints()` calls** — the builder handles it

## Benefits

- **Hard to forget**: new graphs copy existing ones as templates → channel context comes for free
- **Single responsibility**: constraint logic lives in one place (the builder)
- **Future-proof**: when web push notifications are added for proactive messages, channel becomes dynamic instead of hardcoded

## Files involved

- `src/agent/ben/graph/common/system-message-builder.ts` — extend with channel context
- `src/agent/ben/graph/common/whatsapp-constraints.ts` — integrate into builder
- `src/agent/ben/graph/conversation/nodes.ts` — migrate to builder
- `src/agent/ben/graph/training/nodes.ts` — migrate to builder
- `src/agent/ben/graph/youb-expert/nodes.ts` — add channel constraints via builder
- `src/agent/ben/graph/activity/nodes.ts` — add channel constraints via builder
- `src/agent/ben/graph/wakeup/nodes.ts` — add channel constraints via builder
- Supervisor/subgraph state types — rename `source` → `channel` (or add `channel` alongside)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

content.body:
## Summary

Unify the floating glass control elements across Calendar, Training, and Chat into a single consistent "Command Center" pattern. When users swipe between screens, the glass container stays fixed in place — only its inner controls change contextually.

## What

Currently we have three separate implementations of glass-style floating controls:

| Screen | File | Controls |
|--------|------|----------|
| Calendar | `CalendarContainer.vue` | CALENDAR / PLAN segmented toggle |
| Training | `NativeActivitiesContainer.vue` | TRAINING DATA / HEALTH DATA segmented toggle |
| Chat | — | No unified control element (suggestions glass exists separately) |

Each screen builds its own glass container with nearly identical styling (`bg-white/20 backdrop-blur-xl border border-white/30 shadow-2xl rounded-[2.5rem]`), but they're independent components with no shared structure.

### Proposed changes:

1. **Extract a shared `NativeCommandCenter` component** — the glass container that floats in a fixed position across all three screens
2. **Keep the container stationary during swipes** — when the user swipes between Calendar/Chat/Training in `MobileThreeScreenNav`, the command center stays pinned while the screen content scrolls underneath
3. **Contextual inner controls:**
   - **Calendar**: Calendar / Plan toggle (as today)
   - **Chat**: The chat input box lives inside the command center — making it always accessible and visually consistent
   - **Training**: Training Data / Health Data toggle (as today)
4. **Smooth control transitions** — when swiping, the inner controls crossfade or slide to match the active screen

## Why

- **Consistency**: Athletes build muscle memory — same position, same glass style, same interaction pattern on every screen
- **Premium feel**: A persistent, contextual control surface feels like a native iOS command center — it signals "this app was designed, not assembled"
- **Discoverability**: The chat input becoming part of the same visual language as the toggles makes it feel like one coherent tool, not separate features
- **Less code duplication**: The glass container styling is currently copy-pasted across 3 files with minor variations
- **Foundation for extensibility**: Future controls (e.g., quick actions, mode switches) slot naturally into the same surface

## Design Notes

- The command center should be positioned at the **bottom** of the viewport, thumb-reachable
- Needs to respect safe areas (home indicator, etc.)
- Consider subtle haptic feedback when controls change during swipe
- The chat input integration needs careful thought — it expands/collapses differently than a toggle

## Files to touch

- `components/native/MobileThreeScreenNav.vue` — anchor the command center here
- `components/calendar/CalendarContainer.vue` — extract glass control
- `components/native/activities/NativeActivitiesContainer.vue` — extract glass control
- New: `components/native/base/NativeCommandCenter.vue` — shared glass container
- Chat input component — integrate into command center

## Parent Issue
Part of #159 — Android Play Store submission

## What's Needed

### 1. App Icon (512×512 px)
- **Format**: PNG or JPEG, max 1 MB
- **Where it's used**: The main app icon shown in the Play Store listing, search results, and "Top Charts". This is the first thing users see.
- **Requirements**: Must follow [Google's icon design specs](https://developer.android.com/distribute/google-play/resources/icon-design-specifications) — no transparency, no rounded corners (Google applies the mask automatically), should work at small sizes.

### 2. Feature Graphic (1024×500 px, landscape)
- **Format**: PNG or JPEG, max 15 MB
- **Where it's used**: The wide banner at the top of the Play Store listing page. On some devices it appears behind the "Install" button, and it's also used when the app gets featured/promoted. Think of it as a hero image or billboard for the app.
- **Content suggestions**: YOUB logo, tagline (e.g. "Your AI Endurance Coach"), maybe a subtle visual of the app UI or training-related imagery. Should communicate the app's purpose at a glance. Keep text minimal — it may be displayed small.

Both assets need to align with the YOUB brand (dark/premium feel, athlete-focused).

<!-- triage-start -->
## 🔍 Triage

**Type:** chore · **Priority:** high · **Area:** frontend · **Complexity:** simple

### Summary
The project requires an App Icon (512x512) and a Feature Graphic (1024x500) to meet Google Play Store requirements. These assets must align with the Youb brand's dark, premium aesthetic.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] App Icon (512x512 px) is generated and added, following Google's design specs (no transparency, no rounded corners).
- [ ] Feature Graphic (1024x500 px) is generated and added, effectively showcasing the Youb brand and app purpose.
- [ ] Both files are under the maximum file size requirements (1 MB for icon, 15 MB for feature graphic).

### Technical Context
The files should be placed in the project's assets directory or public folder, likely requiring updates to documentation or project configuration for store metadata.

> Use an image generation tool or professional design software to create assets matching the 'dark/premium' brand identity. Save them in the appropriate /assets/ or /public/ directory as defined by project conventions.

content.body:
## Problem

The supervisor uses LLM-based classification (`routing.node.ts`) to route messages to one of four agents: **training**, **activity**, **conversation**, **youb_expert**. Routing is currently **one-shot and one-way** — once a message is dispatched, the chosen agent must handle it regardless of fit.

When the routing is wrong, the agent lacks the tools and context to help, leading to confused responses or hallucinated answers. There is no mechanism for an agent to say "this isn't my domain" and hand back control.

### Examples of misrouting
- User asks about syncing Garmin data → routed to `conversation` instead of `activity`
- User asks to change their training plan mid-conversation about a workout → routed to `activity` instead of `training`
- Ambiguous messages like "can you help me with my plan" (training plan? subscription plan? → `youb_expert` vs `training`)

## Proposed solution: Supervisor-mediated re-routing

Based on industry patterns ([Google ADK multi-agent docs](https://google.github.io/adk-docs/agents/multi-agents/), [LangGraph supervisor](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph-supervisor-py), [Azure agent orchestration patterns](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns)), the **centralized supervisor re-routing** pattern is the best fit for our architecture because:

- We already have a supervisor with full visibility of all agents
- It keeps agent-to-agent coupling at zero (agents don't need to know about each other)
- It's simpler to reason about and debug than peer-to-peer delegation
- It matches LangGraph's native supervisor pattern where control always flows back through the orchestrator

### How it works

1. **Give each agent a `delegate_to_supervisor` tool** — when an agent determines it cannot handle the request, it calls this tool with a `reason` (why it can't help) and an optional `suggestedRoute`
2. **Supervisor receives the delegation with context** — reads the delegation reason, re-classifies the message with that context (plus the failed route excluded), and dispatches to the next best agent
3. **Cap re-routing at 1 re-route** — to prevent infinite loops. If the second agent also can't handle it, fall back to `conversation` as a general-purpose catch-all

### Flow

```
User message
  → Supervisor (routing.node.ts) classifies → Agent A
  → Agent A realizes mismatch → calls delegate_to_supervisor(reason: "user wants to
    modify their training plan, I only have activity analysis tools")
  → Supervisor re-routes with context: original message + delegation reason + excluded routes
  → Agent B handles the request → response
```

### When should an agent delegate?

Agents use **Option A: self-delegation** based on a clear heuristic:

> **Delegate when you have no tools to fulfill the request and the answer isn't general knowledge within your domain.**

The key insight is that agents don't need to know what other agents can do — they just need to recognize when *they* can't help. Tool availability is a concrete, unambiguous signal.

Examples:
- `youb_expert` gets asked to create a training plan → has no plan creation tools → delegates
- `activity` gets asked to modify next week's schedule → has no plan editing tools → delegates
- `conversation` gets asked about syncing Garmin → has no sync/device tools → delegates

The alternative (supervisor reviews every response) was considered but rejected — it adds an extra LLM call to 100% of requests to catch ~5-10% misroutes, which isn't worth the latency/cost tradeoff.

## Tasks

- [ ] Add a `delegate_to_supervisor` tool available to all 4 routed agents — accepts `reason` (string) and optional `suggestedRoute`
- [ ] Extend supervisor graph with a conditional edge back from subgraph to routing — pass the delegation reason into the re-routing prompt so the supervisor can make an informed decision
- [ ] Add `excludeRoutes` param to routing node to skip previously-tried agents
- [ ] Add re-route counter to state (max 1 re-route)
- [ ] Update agent system prompts to instruct when to delegate: "If the user's request requires tools you don't have and the answer isn't general knowledge in your domain, call delegate_to_supervisor with a reason"
- [ ] Add tracing/logging for re-routes to monitor routing quality
- [ ] Expand routing LLM test scenarios to cover delegation edge cases: misrouted messages, ambiguous intents, mid-conversation topic switches, and verify that the full route → delegate → re-route flow produces correct final routing

## Alternatives considered

**Peer-to-peer delegation (agents know about other agents):** Rejected — introduces tight coupling, requires each agent to understand other agents' capabilities, makes routing logic distributed and harder to debug.

**Supervisor reviews every response (Option B):** Rejected as default — extra LLM call on every response adds latency/cost for all users to catch a minority of misroutes. Could be added later as an optional quality layer.

**Confidence-based routing (supervisor routes to top-2 agents):** Complementary improvement to routing quality but doesn't solve the fundamental problem of an agent being stuck with a request it can't handle.

## References
- [Google ADK multi-agent patterns](https://google.github.io/adk-docs/agents/multi-agents/)
- [LangGraph Supervisor library](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph-supervisor-py)
- [Azure AI agent orchestration patterns](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/ai-ml/guide/ai-agent-design-patterns)
- [Patronus AI — Agent routing best practices](https://www.patronus.ai/ai-agent-development/ai-agent-routing)
- [Why multi-agent LLM systems fail (Galileo)](https://galileo.ai/blog/multi-agent-llm-systems-fail)

## Bug Report

**Reported by:** User 876 (phone +436508112120) on 2026-03-14

### Description

Activities are being imported twice — once from Garmin Connect and once from Strava. The user wants:
- **Cycling** data imported only from **Garmin Connect**
- **Running** data imported only from **Strava**

### User message (original, German)

> Hi! Kannst du dir merken, dass du aktuell radfahrtrainings nur von Garmin connect die daten holst und Lauftrainings ausschließlich von strava! Du importierst gerade die trainings doppelt (gleiche trainings von strava und garmin). Kannst du bitte die bisherig gespeicherten trainings jeweils eines löschen?

### Expected behavior

When a user has multiple wearable sources connected (e.g. Garmin + Strava), the system should deduplicate activities — either by detecting duplicates at import time or by allowing per-sport data source preferences.

content.body:
## Bug

User Franziska Hartl (id: 1008) reports updated training sessions are not syncing to Garmin Connect. All 72 sync records are `failed` with error `workoutId X doesn't match with null`.

## Root cause

The Garmin `PUT /training-api/workout/v2/{workoutId}` endpoint requires `workoutId` in the request body matching the URL parameter. Our `GarminWorkoutPayloadSchema` doesn't include it, so the body has `null` → Garmin returns HTTP 400 `BadRequestException`.

## Evidence

Session 7277 was created successfully at 08:32:50 and the first update failed at 08:40:19 (8 min later). Updates have never worked.

## Secondary issue

When updates fail, sync records are permanently stuck in `failed` with no recovery. We need a recovery path for genuinely externally-deleted workouts.

## Fix

1. Include `workoutId` in the PUT request body in `garmin-training-client.service.ts`
2. Add recovery path in `garmin-workout-sync.service.ts` — when update fails with "workout gone" error, delete stale sync record and fall through to create path

content.body:
## Problem

When a user disconnects/reconnects Garmin multiple times, workouts get deleted on Garmin's side but stale sync records remain in our DB with old Garmin workout IDs. On re-sync, `syncSessionUpdate` finds existing sync records, tries to UPDATE non-existent workouts, and gets **404 Not Found**.

However, `isWorkoutGoneError` only matches **400** status codes with a specific message — it doesn't recognize 404. The 404s fall through to the outer catch and permanently mark records as `failed`.

## Root cause

The 400 "doesn't match with null" error was caused by a missing `workoutId` in the PUT body, fixed in `e76d7d69`. That error no longer occurs, so the 400 check is dead code. The actual error from Garmin when a workout doesn't exist is a **404**.

## Fix

Simplify `isWorkoutGoneError` to match **404** status codes instead of the dead 400 check. The existing recovery logic (delete stale sync record → fall through to create path) handles the rest.

## Impact

Affects user 1008 (frh.frg@gmx.de) who has 71 failed sessions. After fix, admin resync will recover them.
content.title: Fix: Garmin workout sync 404 handling marks sessions as permanently failed

content.body:
## Problem

**Trace**: `395fbe33a25cc41cffb68ac9164e9a97` (User 628, Mar 20)

The agent told user 628 they ran 10 km on March 13, but the actual run was 5.95 km. The agent called `get_daily_summaries` which returned `Distanz: 10.46 km` — the **total daily movement** (walking + running + steps). The agent used this number as if it were the run distance.

## Root Causes

1. **Ambiguous label**: The `Distanz` field in daily summaries doesn't clarify it's a daily aggregate, not a single activity distance.
2. **Duplicate rows per day** (Garmin only): Garmin pushes incremental updates with different `summaryId` values, creating multiple `DailySummary` rows per calendar date (e.g., 4 rows for Mar 13 showing 0.01km → 0.12km → 9.81km → 10.46km). This floods the LLM context and adds confusion.
3. **Hardcoded "Garmin" header**: The formatter says "Garmin daily Daten:" but is also used for Suunto data.

## Fix

1. Deduplicate daily summaries by calendar date (keep latest record per date)
2. Rename `Distanz` label to clarify it's an aggregate
3. Make the header provider-agnostic

content.body:
## Problem

Memory-heavy job handlers (FIT file parsing, activity analysis, LLM plan extraction) run in the same process as the HTTP API. An OOM or CPU spike in a job processor takes down the entire API + chat layer.

## Solution

Run job handlers in a separate worker dyno while keeping job enqueueing in the API process.

- Create `PgBossConnectionModule` — provides pg-boss connection + `PgBossService` without `HandlerScannerService`
- API uses `PgBossConnectionModule` (enqueue only, no handler scanning)
- Worker uses the full `PgBossModule` (with handler scanning)
- New `worker.ts` entrypoint + `WorkerModule` with minimal imports

## Files

| File | Action |
|------|--------|
| `src/jobs/pgboss-connection.module.ts` | Create — pg-boss connection without handler scanning |
| `src/jobs/worker-jobs.module.ts` | Create — full pg-boss with handler scanning for worker |
| `src/jobs/jobs.module.ts` | Modify — swap PgBossModule → PgBossConnectionModule |
| `src/worker.module.ts` | Create — worker root module |
| `src/worker.ts` | Create — worker entrypoint |
| `Procfile` | Modify — add worker line |
| `package.json` | Modify — add start:worker script |

content.body:
## Description

The fitness forecast chart shows planned activities with significantly lower load scores compared to actual completed activities. This makes the forecast unreliable for training load planning.

## Root Causes

### 1. Sessions with null workout structure contribute 0 load
Some planned sessions (e.g., "Langer GA1 Lauf 20km", 110 min) have a `null` workout_structure. Since `computePlannedLoad` requires a valid structure, these sessions contribute **0 load** to the forecast. A comparable actual long run produces ~180 load.

### 2. Distance-based intervals are dropped entirely
`getPartDurationSeconds()` in `planned-load.ts` only resolves `durationMinutes` or `durationSeconds`. When a workout part specifies only `distanceMeters` (e.g., "4x 1000m" or "100m strides"), the segment is skipped. This drops the **highest-intensity parts** of interval workouts — the Z5 work intervals that contribute the most load.

**Examples:**
- "4x 1000m Intervals": Z5 work intervals skipped → **38.6** instead of ~70
- "Easy Run + 5 Strides": Z5 strides skipped → **48.8** instead of ~55+

### 3. (Minor) Zone midpoint intensity is slightly conservative
The Z2 midpoint IF of 0.813 is ~8% lower than this user's actual easy run IF (~0.85), leading to a ~9% underestimate on IF² for all Z2 segments.

## Suggested Fixes

1. **Null structures**: Fall back to estimating load from `duration_minutes` + an assumed average intensity (e.g., Z2 for easy/endurance sessions based on title/category)
2. **Distance-only intervals**: Estimate duration from distance + zone pace (e.g., 1000m at Z5 pace ≈ 4 min using `RUNNING_ZONE_PACE_RANGES` and the user's threshold pace)

## Files

- `packages/sport-intelligence/src/common/planned-load.ts` — `getPartDurationSeconds()` and `computePlannedLoad()`
- `src/training/services/fitness.service.ts` — `getFitnessForecast()`
content.title: Fitness forecast underestimates load scores

content.body:
The fitness chart (`GET /api/training/fitness`) and the forecast seed (`POST /api/training/fitness/forecast`) compute different CTL/ATL/TSB for "today" because they use different EMA warmup windows. The chart warmup was `from - 42 days`, while `getSeedValues` called `getFitnessMetrics(today, today)` giving only 42 warmup days. With CTL time constant 42, 42 days of warmup is only ~64% converged vs ~99% with 180+ days.

## Fix
- Replace `WARMUP_DAYS = 42` with `EMA_WARMUP_DAYS = 180`
- Extract shared `computeDailyFitness` method anchored to `to` date
- Refactor both `getFitnessMetrics` and `getSeedValues` to use the shared method
- Add E2E consistency test verifying forecast seeds match fitness endpoint

content.body:
## Problem

We have three separate zone tables (`user_running_zones`, `user_heart_rate_zones`, `user_power_zones`) that store pre-computed zone boundaries. These are 100% derivable from `athletic_profile` fields (`threshold_pace`, `ftp`, `max_heart_rate`) using the `calculateRunningZones()`, `calculatePowerZones()`, and `calculateHeartRateZones()` functions in `@youbdev/sport-intelligence`.

The zone rows are created via `athletic-profile.service.ts` `updateProfile()`, but this path was never triggered for existing users — **0 out of 67 users with athletic profiles had zone rows**. This caused Garmin workout exports to silently fall back to `targetType: 'OPEN'` (no pace/power targets), which is the root cause of Thorsten's reported missing workout data.

## Proposed Change

1. **Remove the three zone tables** (`user_running_zones`, `user_heart_rate_zones`, `user_power_zones`)
2. **Compute zones on-the-fly** wherever they're needed, using the existing `calculate*Zones()` functions from `athletic_profile` fields
3. **Update consumers** — primarily:
   - `garmin-workout-mapper.service.ts` — `mapZoneToTarget()` reads `zones.runningZones` / `zones.powerZones`
   - `workout-structure-enricher.ts` — `resolvePaceTarget()` takes `UserRunningZones`
   - `user-zones.service.ts` — can be simplified to just expose `calculate*` functions
   - `user-zones.repository.ts` — can be removed
4. **Migration** to drop the three tables

## Context

- Related to Garmin sync quality issues reported by users (missing pace/distance targets)
- Thorsten (user 1005) was manually patched with zone rows as a stopgap
- The `paceRange` and `ftpRange` fields on workout structure parts are already stored as percentages of threshold — the mapper just needs the threshold value itself, not pre-computed boundaries
content.title: Eliminate zone tables — derive zones from athletic profile

## Problem

The Strava activity analysis service does not persist `loadScore` for running activities. The cycling path sets `loadScore: analysisResult.powerMetrics?.tss`, but the running path omits it entirely.

This means any Strava-connected user's running activities will have NULL `loadScore` in `activity_analysis`, causing them to be excluded from the fitness API weekly totals.

## Root Cause

In `src/wearable/strava/strava-activity-analysis.service.ts`, the running analysis persist block (~line 198) never sets `loadScore`. Compare:

- **Cycling** (line 117): `loadScore: analysisResult.powerMetrics?.tss ?? null` ✅
- **Running** (~line 198): `loadScore` not set ❌

The common `ActivityAnalysisService` used by Garmin/Suunto/Wahoo correctly sets `loadScore: analysisResult.runMetrics?.tss` for running — this is only missing in the Strava-specific service.

## Fix

Add `loadScore: analysisResult.runMetrics?.tss ?? null` to the Strava running analysis persist block, then reanalyze affected activities.

content.body:
## Bug

Garmin workout exports for cycling sessions have **no power targets** — all steps are exported as `targetType: OPEN`. The user sees durations on their watch but no wattage guidance, defeating the purpose of structured workouts.

**Observed in production** for user 1008 (frh.frg@gmx.de) who has FTP=185 in their athletic profile.

### Root cause

Power zones are stored in a separate `user_power_zones` table, which is only populated when `ENABLE_USER_ZONES=true` (feature flag, currently **off** in production). So even though the user has FTP set, the Garmin mapper can't resolve watt targets and falls back to OPEN.

The same architecture applies to HR zones and running zones — all are materialized caches of pure functions (`calculatePowerZones(ftp)`, `calculateHeartRateZones(maxHr)`, `calculateRunningZones(thresholdPace)`) gated behind the same feature flag.

### Proposed fix

Derive zones dynamically from `athletic_profile` fields instead of reading from separate tables:

- `powerZones` = `calculatePowerZones(ftp)` — pure function from `@youbdev/sport-intelligence`
- `heartRateZones` = `calculateHeartRateZones(maxHr)` — same
- `runningZones` = `calculateRunningZones(thresholdPace)` — same

Then remove:
- `user_power_zones`, `user_heart_rate_zones`, `user_running_zones` tables
- `ENABLE_USER_ZONES` feature flag
- The zone upsert/sync machinery in `athletic-profile.service.ts`

### Logs

Production logs during Garmin resync show hundreds of warnings:
```
WARN [GarminWorkoutMapperService] Session 9999: cycling step has no ftpRange or power zones, falling back to OPEN target
```

### Secondary issue

Some workout parts also have **missing durations** (warmups, cooldowns, intervals exported as open-ended):
```
WARN [GarminWorkoutMapperService] Workout part missing duration, creating OPEN step: Gleichmäßiges Z2 Einfahren
```
This is a plan generation quality issue — the LLM-generated workout structure omits `durationMinutes` on some parts.

content.body:
## Problem

The Garmin workout mapper produces suboptimal targets in two cases:

### 1. Z6 (anaerobic) intervals have `targetType: OPEN` — no pace guidance on watch

Z6 is defined with `fast: null` (unbounded upper end). When the workout part has no explicit `paceRange` and falls back to zone lookup, Z6 resolves to `fast: null` which the mapper treats as "no target available" → exports as `targetType: OPEN`.

**Example**: Thorsten's "8x 200m Speed Work" — the 200m sprint intervals show up on Garmin with no pace target at all. The athlete gets zero guidance during the hard effort.

**Expected**: At minimum export a one-sided pace target (`targetValueLow` only, no ceiling), e.g. "faster than Z5 fast boundary".

### 2. Recovery/Z1 power targets have `targetValueLow: 0`

Recovery steps in cycling workouts export `targetValueLow: 0.0` watts. While technically correct (Z1 = 0-55% FTP), a zero-watt floor is meaningless guidance. Similarly the "Lange Fahrt 180min" has `targetValueLow: 0.0` for a 3-hour aerobic ride.

**Expected**: Use a reasonable floor (e.g. Z1 min or at least some non-zero value).

## Found in

- Thorsten's "8x 200m Speed Work" — `targetType: OPEN` on sprint intervals
- Franzi's recovery rides and long ride — `targetValueLow: 0.0`

## Files

- `src/wearable/garmin/garmin-workout-mapper.service.ts` — `mapZoneToTarget()`
- `packages/sport-intelligence/src/zones/zone-constants.ts` — zone definitions

content.body:
## Problem

The `estimatedDurationInSecs` field on exported Garmin workouts doesn't match the actual sum of step durations. The field appears to come from `session.durationMinutes` (the planned session duration) rather than being calculated from the workout steps.

**Examples** (Franzi's workouts):

| Workout | estimatedDuration | Actual step sum | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5x 3min VO2max | 3240s (54min) | 2700s (45min) | +9min |
| Lange Fahrt + 4x 4min | 4020s (67min) | 3480s (58min) | +9min |
| Lange Fahrt + 6x 4min | 4980s (83min) | 4320s (72min) | +11min |

## Impact

Low — Garmin uses individual step durations for guidance, not the estimated total. But it may show incorrect total time in the Garmin Connect calendar preview.

## Fix

Calculate `estimatedDurationInSecs` from the sum of all step `durationValue` fields (including repeat steps × repetitions) instead of using `session.durationMinutes * 60`.

## Files

- `src/wearable/garmin/garmin-workout-mapper.service.ts` — `mapSessionToGarminWorkout()`

content.body:
## Problem

Several quality issues found when auditing Garmin-exported workouts generated by the AI coach:

### 1. Progressive workouts exported as single flat step

Thorsten's "Progressive Tempo 6km" is a single step with an easy Z2 pace target for the entire 6km. A progressive run by definition should escalate through zones (e.g. Z3 → Z4 → HM pace). The workout structure should contain multiple steps with increasing pace targets.

Also `estimatedDurationInSecs=1200` (20min) seems too short for 6km at his pace (~286 s/km threshold → ~29min at threshold).

### 2. "Lange Fahrt" title on short interval sessions

Franzi has two workouts titled "Lange Fahrt + Nx4min Schwellenarbeit" that are 67min and 83min respectively. "Lange Fahrt" (long ride) implies 2-3+ hours. These are structured interval sessions with an extended warmup — naming them "long ride" is misleading.

### 3. Step descriptions say Z1 when workout describes Z2

Thorsten's easy/endurance runs (Long Run, Easy Run, DL2) all have step descriptions saying "lockerer Dauerlauf (Z1)" while the workout descriptions reference Z2 effort. Z1 is recovery, Z2 is endurance — these are different training zones.

### 4. Duplicate identical recovery workout names

Franzi has 4 recovery rides that are byte-for-byte identical in name, description, duration, and targets. In the Garmin workout library this creates confusion — the user sees 4 entries called "Recovery Ride" with no way to distinguish which is for which day.

**Suggestion**: Append the date or a differentiator, e.g. "Recovery Ride (Di)" or "Recovery Ride (26.03)".

## Impact

These are plan generation quality issues from the LLM, not sync/mapper bugs. They affect the athlete's experience when reading workouts on their watch or in Garmin Connect.

content.body:
## Summary

Introduce a first-class `RaceGoal` model so users can track race goals independently of training plans. Currently, race info (raceDate, raceName, etc.) is embedded as fields on `TrainingPlan`, which limits us:

- A goal can exist before any plan is created
- Multiple plans can serve one goal (base → build → peak phases)
- The coach agent needs to see upcoming races to periodize training, taper correctly, and schedule around race days
- Post-race results live on the matched activity, not duplicated on the plan

## Design

### `RaceGoal` model

```prisma
enum RaceGoalPriority {
  A    // full taper + peak
  B    // partial taper
  C    // train through
}

model RaceGoal {
  id            Int              @id @default(autoincrement())
  userId        Int
  user          User             @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])

  eventName     String           // "Linz Marathon 2026"
  eventDate     DateTime         // race day
  eventDistance  String?          // "marathon", "10k", "olympic-triathlon"
  targetTime    String?          // "3:00:00" — used by coach for pacing
  priority      RaceGoalPriority @default(A)
  notes         String?

  sessions      RaceGoalsOnTrainingSessions[]

  createdAt     DateTime         @default(now())
  updatedAt     DateTime         @updatedAt
}

model RaceGoalsOnTrainingSessions {
  raceGoalId        Int
  raceGoal          RaceGoal        @relation(fields: [raceGoalId], references: [id])
  trainingSessionId Int
  trainingSession   TrainingSession @relation(fields: [trainingSessionId], references: [id])

  @@id([raceGoalId, trainingSessionId])
}
```

### Key decisions

- **Named `RaceGoal`, not `Goal`** — explicit about what it is. Other goal types (volume, performance) get their own models later
- **No status field** — derivable from `eventDate` vs now. Deletion = abandonment
- **No sport field** — `eventDistance` implies sport; avoids redundancy with plan categories
- **No `actualTime`** — race results come from the matched activity, not duplicated
- **Connected to `TrainingSession` (M:N)** — the race is a session in a plan (already has `isRace` flag). Plans reachable via `goal.sessions[].trainingSession.trainingPlan`
- **A/B/C priority** — classic periodization. A-race gets full taper, B partial, C trained through. Critical for coach agent

### Migration path

Existing `TrainingPlan` fields (`raceDate`, `raceName`, `raceDistance`, `raceGoalTime`) remain for now. New code writes to `RaceGoal`. Backfill existing data in a migration. Deprecate old fields later.

## Scope

- [ ] Prisma schema + migration
- [ ] RaceGoal CRUD (repository, service, controller, DTOs)
- [ ] Expose goals to coach agent context
- [ ] Backfill migration for existing plan race data
- [ ] Connect plan creation flow to use RaceGoal instead of inline race fields

## Future (out of scope)

- Other goal types (VolumeGoal, PerformanceGoal, etc.)
- Goal-to-activity direct linking (race result tracking)
content.title: Add RaceGoal model for structured goal tracking

## Problem

Recovery rides (and likely other simple sessions) have `workout_structure = null` in the DB. When exported to Garmin, the mapper falls through to a simple single-step workout with `targetType: OPEN` - no power target on the watch.

Example: Franzi (user 1008) has 4 Recovery Rides all exported as OPEN with no description and no power target.

```sql
SELECT ts.id, ts.title, ts.workout_structure 
FROM training_session ts WHERE ts.id = 10007;
-- workout_structure is NULL
```

## Expected

Recovery rides should have a `workoutStructure` with a Z1 zone, so the mapper can resolve a power target (e.g. 0-102W for FTP 185).

## Root Cause

The plan generator does not create a `workoutStructure` for simple single-zone sessions like recovery rides. The mapper's simple workout path (`session.durationMinutes` only) doesn't set any target.

## Fix Options

1. Plan generator: always create a `workoutStructure` even for simple sessions (with a single mainSet work entry including the zone)
2. Mapper fallback: when no structure exists for cycling, use a default Z1 power target based on the session title/type

## Problem

Polyline (map route) data is computed on every `/api/training/activities/:id/details` request by filtering GPS records, LTTB downsampling to 2500 points, and encoding with `@mapbox/polyline`. This is wasteful — the GPS data doesn't change between requests. It also means the seed sync can't include map data since it's never persisted.

## Solution

Add a `polyline` column to `activity_analysis` and compute it once during analysis:

- **Garmin**: Extract from FIT records after parsing
- **Strava**: Compute from stream GPS data during analysis  
- **Details endpoint**: Use stored polyline with fallback to on-the-fly computation
- **Backfill**: Batch process existing activities from persisted `records` JSON (no S3 access needed)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New `polyline` nullable text column on `activity_analysis`
- [ ] Polyline computed and stored during Garmin FIT analysis
- [ ] Polyline computed and stored during Strava stream analysis
- [ ] Details endpoint uses stored polyline (falls back to computed if null)
- [ ] Seed sync exports/imports polyline column
- [ ] Backfill script for existing activities

content.body:
## Problem

Date-only fields (`eventDate` on RaceGoal, `raceDate`/`startDate`/`endDate` on TrainingPlan) are stored as `TIMESTAMP(3)` at UTC midnight (`2026-04-12T00:00:00Z`). When these are converted to the user's timezone for display via `utcToLocal`, the date can shift (e.g. for UTC- timezones, midnight UTC becomes the previous day).

Currently some paths use `toISOString().split('T')[0]` (no timezone awareness) and others use `utcToLocal` — inconsistent and fragile.

## Solution

Store date-only values at **noon in the user's timezone** (converted to UTC). This guarantees that `utcToLocal` on read always returns the correct calendar date regardless of timezone (noon ± 14 hours never crosses a day boundary).

### Code changes
- Add a `dateOnlyToUtc(dateStr, timezone)` utility that creates noon in the user's timezone
- Update all date-only write paths (race goal create/update, training plan create) to use it
- Update all date-only read paths to consistently use `utcToLocal` instead of `toISOString().split('T')[0]`

### Data migration
- Update staging date-only fields from midnight UTC to noon in the user's timezone

## Affected fields
- `RaceGoal.eventDate`
- `TrainingPlan.raceDate`, `startDate`, `endDate`

content.body:
## Problem

Health data tables have massive duplication — multiple records per user per day instead of one.

**Local dev DB (single user):**

| Table | Total records | Unique days | Avg per day | Max per day |
|-------|--------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| daily_summary | 830 | 128 | ~6.5 | 16 |
| stress_detail | 863 | 129 | ~6.7 | 16 |
| respiration_record | 11,859 | 0 (all NULL) | — | — |

Respiration records have **no calendar_date populated at all** (all NULL).

## Root Cause

### 1. Unique constraint is too narrow

All three tables use `@@unique([provider, summaryId])`. This prevents duplicate summaryIds but **not** duplicate records for the same user + calendar date. Garmin sends multiple summaryIds per day (morning, afternoon, evening pushes), and each one creates a new row.

### 2. Garmin webhook handlers use CREATE, not UPSERT

In `garmin-webhook.service.ts`:
- `processDailies()` → calls `createSummary()` (plain CREATE)
- `processStress()` → calls `createStress()` (plain CREATE)
- `processRespiration()` → calls `createRespiration()` (plain CREATE)

The existing dedup check (`findBySummaryId`) only prevents the exact same summaryId from being inserted twice — it doesn't consolidate multiple summaryIds for the same day.

By contrast, Suunto's handler uses `upsertSummary()` with a synthetic key `suunto:${user.id}:${calendarDate}`, guaranteeing one record per user per day.

### 3. Respiration has no calendar_date logic

Respiration records are created without populating `calendar_date`, making them impossible to deduplicate or query by date.

## Proposed Fix

1. **Change unique constraint** to `@@unique([provider, userId, calendarDate])` (or add it as a secondary constraint) for all three tables
2. **Switch Garmin handlers from CREATE to UPSERT** keyed on `(provider, userId, calendarDate)` — latest data for the day wins
3. **Populate calendar_date on respiration records** from the `startTimeInSeconds` field
4. **Write a data migration** to deduplicate existing rows (keep the most recent per user+date, delete the rest)
5. **Add calendar_date index** on respiration_record if not already present

## Impact

- DB bloat: ~6-16x more rows than necessary per user per day
- Queries aggregating daily health data may return inflated/incorrect values
- Will get worse as more users connect Garmin devices

Parent epic: YoubDev/issues#50

content.body:
## Problem

Calendar sync polling runs every 10 minutes and re-fetches + re-upserts ALL events for ALL users every cycle. With ~8,700 calendar events but ~4.9M DB calls, each event is being touched ~563 times. This saturates the Heroku essential-0 connection pool (20/20) and consumes 76.3% of total DB execution time.

## Root Causes

1. **No incremental sync** -- every poll re-fetches all events from Google and re-upserts them, even when nothing changed
2. **Individual upserts** -- each event is a separate Prisma upsert call (500 events = 500 DB round trips per user)
3. **Unconditional conflict detection** -- runs after every sync regardless of changes, querying all events to year 2099
4. **Connection pool not sized** -- Prisma defaults + pg-boss can exceed Heroku's 20 connection limit

## Fix

1. Incremental sync with Google Calendar syncToken (already used for training polling)
2. Batch upserts with raw SQL INSERT ON CONFLICT
3. Conditional conflict detection (skip when no changes)
4. Bound conflict detection query to 90 days instead of year 2099
5. Connection pool configuration
content.title: fix: calendar sync hot path consuming 76% DB time with ~4.9M calls

content.body:
## Problem

When a user asks about their training zones ("Kannst du mir meine Trainingszonen zeigen?"), the agent fails to present them:

1. **Routing**: "Trainingszonen" gets routed to `training` (plan management), which doesn't have `get_athletic_profile`. After delegation, it re-routes to `youb_expert` (feature docs search) instead of `conversation` (which has the athletic profile tool).
2. **Feature docs**: `settings-biometric-data.md` doesn't explicitly state that zone percentage ranges are fixed and NOT manually adjustable, causing the agent to hallucinate a "Trainingszonen" settings page.

## Expected Behavior

- Zone queries route to `conversation` subgraph which has `get_athletic_profile`
- Agent presents computed zones with numeric ranges from the user's profile
- Agent correctly explains zones cannot be manually adjusted (percentages are fixed)

## Root Cause

- Routing prompt (`routing.systemPrompt`) doesn't mention athletic profile, training zones, or biometric data under `conversation` category
- Feature docs lack explicit statement that zone boundaries are system-defined and not user-editable

content.body:
## Summary

Migrate from the current (eval/dev) Garmin application to the new production-level Garmin application. Run both apps in parallel during the transition so existing users are not disrupted.

## Approach

Run old and new Garmin apps in parallel:
- **New/reconnecting users** authenticate against the new production app
- **Existing users** keep working on old tokens until they reconnect
- Connection status returns `reconnectRecommended: true` for legacy users so the frontend can prompt them
- Token refresh uses the correct credentials based on which app the user is on
- Webhooks accept both old and new `Garmin-Client-Id` headers

## Implementation

1. **DB migration**: Add `garmin_app_version` smallint column (default 1 = legacy, 2 = new)
2. **Config**: Add `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_ID` and `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_SECRET` optional env vars
3. **Webhook guard**: Accept both old and new client IDs
4. **Token refresh**: Pick credentials based on user's `garminAppVersion`
5. **OAuth flow**: Set `garminAppVersion: 2` on new connections
6. **Connection status DTO**: Add `reconnectRecommended` field

## Deployment Checklist

### Staging
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_ID` = current staging `GARMIN_CLIENT_ID`
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_SECRET` = current staging `GARMIN_CLIENT_SECRET`
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CLIENT_ID` = new production app client ID
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CLIENT_SECRET` = new production app client secret
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CODE_VERIFIER` = new app's PKCE verifier (if changed)
- [ ] Update `GARMIN_CALLBACK_URL` if needed
- [ ] Run migration: `npx prisma migrate deploy`
- [ ] Verify existing user token refresh still works
- [ ] Verify new OAuth flow works

### Production
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_ID` = current production `GARMIN_CLIENT_ID`
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_LEGACY_CLIENT_SECRET` = current production `GARMIN_CLIENT_SECRET`
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CLIENT_ID` = new production app client ID
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CLIENT_SECRET` = new production app client secret
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CODE_VERIFIER` = new app's PKCE verifier (if changed)
- [ ] Update `GARMIN_CALLBACK_URL` if needed
- [ ] Run migration: `npx prisma migrate deploy`
- [ ] Monitor: `SELECT garmin_app_version, COUNT(*) FROM "user" WHERE garmin_access_token IS NOT NULL GROUP BY garmin_app_version;`

### Local Dev
- [ ] Move current `.env` Garmin values to `GARMIN_LEGACY_*`
- [ ] Set `GARMIN_CLIENT_ID` / `GARMIN_CLIENT_SECRET` to new app values

## Post-Migration Cleanup (separate issue)

Once all users are on version 2, remove legacy config, simplify guard, simplify token refresh.

## Problem

Production DB at 873MB / 1GB limit. Main consumers:
- `activity_analysis` records+laps JSONB: ~550MB (avg 1.1MB per row, 2,265 rows)
- `webhook_log` raw_payload: ~262MB (27,866 rows, write-only — never read back)

At 1,000 users x 3 years this projects to ~1.4TB in Postgres.

## Solution

### Phase 1: Reduce webhook log retention 30 to 7 days (~230MB savings)
One-line change. `raw_payload` is never read back -- pure audit log.

### Phase 2: Move records + laps to S3 (~550MB savings)

- Update 2 read paths (activity details endpoint, admin polyline backfill)

- Fallback: if S3 upload fails, store in DB as before

### Phase 3: Drop JSONB columns (future, separate issue)
After backfill verified + 1 week in production, drop `records` and `laps` columns.

## Expected impact
~780MB immediate savings, DB drops to ~100MB. Projected cost at 1K users: ~$50/mo Postgres + ~$28/mo S3 vs ~$1,200/mo Postgres-only.

content.body:
## Context

A comprehensive code review across `src/`, `test/`, and module architecture revealed recurring anti-patterns that slip through the existing 22 custom ESLint rules. No production bugs were found, but several patterns will cause increasing maintenance pain.

---

## Quick Wins (direct code fixes)

### 1. `planStatus: string` in DomainEventMap should use `PlanStatus` enum
**File**: `src/common/events/domain-events.ts:17,22,28,34-35`

`planStatus`, `oldStatus`, `newStatus` are typed as `string` but carry enum-shaped values (`active`, `preview`, `archived`, `completed`, `abandoned`). The `PlanStatus` enum already exists in `src/training/types/training.types.ts`. 8 listener files compare against untyped string literals - if `PlanStatus` gains a value or one is renamed, TypeScript won't catch the stale comparisons.

### 2. `ScheduleModule.forRoot()` imported twice
**Files**: `src/app.module.ts:81` and `src/auth/auth.module.ts:30`

`AuthModule` imports `ScheduleModule.forRoot()` but it's already registered globally in `AppModule`. Double initialization.

### 3. MessagingModule re-provides global repositories
**File**: `src/messaging/messaging.module.ts:48-53`

`PreferencesRepository`, `ConversationRepository`, `ChatMessageRepository`, `ActivityRepository`, `SleepRepository` are listed in `providers` despite being available via the `@Global()` `RepositoriesModule`. Creates duplicate DI instances.

### 4. 70+ `eslint-disable` for Prisma JSON type assertions
**Pattern**: `as unknown as Prisma.InputJsonValue` scattered across ~20 files (heaviest in `training-plan-instantiation.service.ts` with 8 occurrences). A centralized `toInputJson()` helper would reduce 70+ suppressions to 1.

---

## New Lint Rules (high value)

### 5. `no-error-message-only-in-catch` - 261 occurrences, 98 files
**Pattern**: `error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)` in catch blocks discards stack traces. The correct NestJS approach is passing `error` directly to the logger. Three competing error serialization patterns exist with no consistency.

### 6. `no-class-in-controller` - inline DTO definitions
**Pattern**: `training-plan.controller.ts` defines 3 DTO classes inline, making it 700+ lines. DTOs should live in separate files.

### 7. `prefer-injected-config` - 24 files use raw `ConfigService.get()`
**Pattern**: `this.configService.get<Type>('STRING_KEY')` instead of the typed `@Inject(config.KEY)` + `ConfigType<typeof config>` pattern. Each usage requires `eslint-disable` for `@typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-*`.

---

## Architecture Findings (informational, not in scope for this issue)

These were surfaced by the audit but require larger refactors:

- **Circular dependency web**: 14 `forwardRef()` across 9 modules, setter injection in `AppModule.onModuleInit()`. Best addressed with `dependency-cruiser`.
- **God modules**: `TrainingCoreModule` exports all 18 providers with no encapsulation. `GarminCoreModule` exports 14/17.
- **Duplicate `ActivityRepository`**: one global (`src/repository/`), one local (`src/training/repositories/`), both on the same Prisma model.
- **Test anti-patterns**: 532 `as any` across 57 test files, 100+ `setTimeout` sleep-based synchronization, 481 weak `toBeDefined()` assertions, conditional logic that silently skips assertions.

---

## Test Findings (informational)

| Pattern | Occurrences | Files |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| `as any` in tests | 532 | 57 |
| `as never` in tests | 257 | 21 |
| `as unknown as Mocked<T>` | 92 | 29 |
| `setTimeout` as sync primitive | 100+ | ~20 |
| Weak `toBeDefined()` | 481 | 102 |
| Conditional assertion skips | ~20 | 6 |
content.title: Code health: fix anti-patterns and add lint rules from audit

content.body:
## Problem

The web dyno is crashing with R15 (memory quota vastly exceeded) — spiking from ~400MB to 1.3-1.8GB within 30 seconds of startup. Root cause: the `*/10` cron jobs fire shortly after restart, triggering **28 Google Calendar connections syncing in parallel**, each fetching hundreds of events (one connection alone synced 925 events). This concurrent burst overwhelms the 512MB Basic dyno.

Additionally, Google Training Polling (20 connections) and token refreshes for multiple providers all fire at the same `:00` mark, compounding the spike.

## Evidence

- Web dyno RSS at rest: ~404MB (heap only 134MB)
- After cron fires: 1358-1880MB → R15 → SIGKILL
- No user traffic or heavy requests correlated — pure cron-driven spike
- Worker dyno (which processes FIT files) is already running on its own dyno

## Proposed solution

1. **Move calendar sync cron jobs to the worker process** — `google-calendar-polling`, `google-training-polling`, and related token refresh jobs are batch operations, not request-serving work
2. **Throttle concurrency** — process connections sequentially or with limited concurrency (e.g. 5 at a time) instead of 28 in parallel
3. **Stagger cron schedules** — avoid all jobs firing at the same `:00` mark

## Impact

- Production web dyno crash-looping after every deploy
- Garmin health checks, WhatsApp webhooks, and API requests returning 503 during crash windows
content.title: Move Google Calendar sync to worker and throttle concurrency

One activity for f3l1x on staging (activity 231, Jan 7) produced a load score of 4.72e18 due to the grade adjustment factor in `calculateGASSeries` going negative on very steep descent segments.
